name: app-router
context: Build Next.js apps with the App Router — file-based routing, layouts, Server Components, streaming, and loading states
info: The App Router is Next.js's recommended routing system, built on React Server Components. It uses the `app/` directory with file conventions: page.tsx defines routes, layout.tsx provides shared UI that persists across navigations, loading.tsx creates Suspense boundaries for streaming, error.tsx handles errors with React error boundaries, not-found.tsx renders 404 UI, and template.tsx provides per-navigation re-rendering. Route organization features include route groups `(folder)` for logical grouping without URL impact, dynamic segments `[slug]`, catch-all segments `[...slug]`, optional catch-all `[[...slug]]`, parallel routes `@slot`, and intercepting routes `(.)folder`. Components are Server Components by default — add `'use client'` for interactivity. The App Router supports streaming, partial prerendering, and nested layouts that make complex UIs composable.
triggers: add a route to my next app

sections.title: File conventions
sections.body: The App Router maps files to UI concerns: `page.tsx` defines the unique UI of a route, `layout.tsx` provides shared UI that wraps children and persists across navigations, `loading.tsx` creates a Suspense fallback, `error.tsx` creates an error boundary, `not-found.tsx` renders 404 UI, `template.tsx` re-renders on every navigation unlike layout, `route.ts` defines API endpoints (see [[nextjs/api-routes]]), and `default.tsx` provides fallback content for parallel routes.

sections.title: Dynamic routes
sections.body: Dynamic segments use brackets in folder names: `[slug]` matches one segment, `[...slug]` catches all remaining segments, `[[...slug]]` optionally catches all segments (also matches the route without the parameter). In Next.js 15+, `params` is a Promise that must be awaited: `const { slug } = await params`.

sections.title: Route groups
sections.body: Wrap folder names in parentheses `(name)` to create route groups. Route groups organize files without adding path segments to the URL. Each group can have its own layout. Multiple root layouts are possible by placing `layout.tsx` files in different route groups (remove the top-level `app/layout.tsx` in this case).

sections.title: Parallel routes
sections.body: Define named slots with `@folder` convention. The parent layout receives each slot as a prop. Parallel routes enable rendering multiple pages in the same layout simultaneously — useful for dashboards, modals, and split views. Each slot can have independent loading and error states. Use `default.tsx` to provide fallback content when a slot doesn't match the current URL.

sections.title: Server and client component boundary
sections.body: Components in the App Router are Server Components by default. Add `'use client'` directive at the top of a file to make it a Client Component. Server Components can import Client Components, but Client Components cannot import Server Components. The pattern for composing them is to pass Server Components as `children` or other props to Client Components. Use the `server-only` package to prevent server code from being bundled into client code.

sections.title: Navigation
sections.body: Use the `<Link>` component from `next/link` for declarative navigation with automatic prefetching. For programmatic navigation in Client Components, use `useRouter()` from `next/navigation` with `push()`, `replace()`, `back()`, and `refresh()`. The `redirect()` function from `next/navigation` works in Server Components and Server Actions. Links are automatically prefetched when they enter the viewport.

routeUrl.description: URL path this route maps to
